LAW AND MOTION, 302, THE COURT ADOPTED ITS TENTATIVE RULING. DEFENDANT SHARP CORPORATION'S MOTION FOR LEAVE TO AMEND ANSWER IS DENIED. THE COURT "MAY, IN FURTHERANCE OF JUSTICE, AND ON ANY TERMS AS MAY BE PROPER, ALLOW A PARTY TO AMEND ANY PLEADING . . . ." (CODE CIV. PRO. 473, SUBD. (A)(1).) IN MARCH 2011, DEFENDANTS ALLEGEDLY CONTRIBUTED TO MAILING TO CALIFORNIA PACIFIC MEDICAL CENTER A PACKAGE CONTAINING SODIUM CHLORITE. IN 2011, IT WAS REASONABLE TO INFER THAT A DOCTOR SUCH AS PLAINTIFF MIGHT OPEN THAT PACKAGE. IN 2013, PLAINTIFF FILED THE INSTANT ACTION ON THE BASIS OF ALLEGED PERMANENT EYE INJURY AND PAIN. TRIAL BEGINS IN THREE AND A HALF MONTHS. DEFENDANTS SEEK TO AMEND THEIR ANSWERS BASED ON THE LEGAL THEORY THAT PLAINTIFF'S CLAIMS WERE NARROWED DURING DISCOVERY BECAUSE SHE IDENTIFIED WHICH CHEMICAL ALLEGEDLY CAUSED HER INJURY. DEFENDANTS ALSO ALLEGE THAT PLAINTIFF DELAYED THE FILING OF THE INSTANT MOTION BY INDICATING SIX WEEKS AGO THAT IT WOULD NOT FILE AN OPPOSITION. THESE ARGUMENTS ARE NOT ENTIRELY PERSUASIVE AND PLAINTIFF WOULD EXPERIENCE IMPERMISSIBLE BURDEN IF THE COURT GRANTED DEFENDANTS' MOTION AT THIS STAGE. PREVAILING PARTY TO PREPARE A FORM OF ORDER. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication (Joinder)
Off Calendar Jan-15-2016 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances: Christopher Viadro of Butler Viadro, LLP telephone number 510-287-2400 for Plaintiff.
Michael Pulliam of Drinker Biddle & Reath LLP telephone number 415-591-7634 for Defendant Sharp Corporation.
Joy Chen of Wilson Elsre LLP telephone number 415-433-0990 for Defendant Neuraltus Pharmaceuticals, Inc.

The matter was argued. Upon conclusion of argument, the Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT NEURALTUS PHARMACEUTICALS INC.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, Alternatively, Summary Adjudication (Joinder) is denied. Plaintiff submits sufficient evidence to support a res ipso loquitur theory. (Plaintiff's Additional Material Facts to Sharp Corporations Motion For Summary Judgment AMF 7-8; Plaintiff's Additional Material Facts to Neuraltus Pharmaceuticals, Inc.'s Joinder to Sharp Corporations Motion For Summary Judgment AMF 1-7.) A jury should decide whether Plaintiff can establish negligence based on this theory. Defendants both argue that Plaintiff cannot prevail on the negligence claim because there is not evidence that the spilled investigational drug could evaporate into sodium chlorite powder capable of causing Plaintiff's injuries. Because Defendants rely on equivocal evidence to support the position that it is impossible for Plaintiff to prove that the investigational drug could evaporate into sodium chlorite powder, Defendants fail to maintain their burden of demonstrating that the cause of action cannot be established. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 850; Sharp's UMF 13 and Neuraltus Pharmaceuticals, Inc.'s UMF 17; Dr. Anderson's Deposition 29:13-32:1.) As to the cause of action for negligent failure to warn, summary adjudication is denied. Plaintiff raises a triable issue of material fact as to whether it was foreseeable that the package presented a substantial danger, given that a Medical Safety Data Sheet was in the box with warnings that exposure to the chemical in the drug could cause severe irritation and/or burns. (Plaintiff's Additional Material Facts to Sharp Corporations Motion For Summary Judgment AMF 7-8; Plaintiff's Additional Material Facts to Neuraltus Pharmaceuticals, Inc.'s Joinder to Sharp Corporations Motion For Summary Judgment AMF 7.) The prevailing party is to provide a form of order and comply with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Ernest Goldsmith. Clerk: Rosie Noguera. Reporter: Sherry Sawyer, CSR #5976. (302/EG)

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Leave To Amend Answer
Off Calendar Nov-09-2015 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearance on this matter. The Court adopted its tentative ruling.
DEFENDANT SHARP CORPORATION's Motion For Leave To Amend Answer is DENIED. The Court "may, in furtherance of justice, and on any terms as may be proper, allow a party to amend any pleading . . . ." (Code Civ. Pro. 473, subd. (a)(1).) In March 2011, Defendants allegedly contributed to mailing to California Pacific Medical Center a package containing sodium chlorite. In 2011, it was reasonable to infer that a doctor such as Plaintiff might open that package. In 2013, Plaintiff filed the instant action on the basis of alleged permanent eye injury and pain. Trial begins in three and a half months. Defendants seek to amend their answers based on the legal theory that Plaintiff's claims were narrowed during discovery because she identified which chemical allegedly caused her injury. Defendants also allege that Plaintiff delayed the filing of the instant motion by indicating six weeks ago that it would not file an opposition. These arguments are not entirely persuasive and Plaintiff would experience impermissible burden if the Court granted Defendants' motion at this stage. Prevailing party to prepare a form of order. Ntc Of Continuance Of Hearing On Motion To Quash/Dismiss Demurrer
Off Calendar Dec-23-2013 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: APPEARANCES - ATTY. ALAN J. ZACHARIN, TEL. NO. (510) 508-9702 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. THOMAS M. ROBINS III OF FRANDZEL ROBINS & CSATO, L.C., TEL. NO. (323) 852-1000 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT;
ATTY. AMY FRENZEN OF THE DRINKER BIDDLE & REATH LLP, TEL. NO. 591-7597 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT SHARP CORPORATION BY TELEPHONE APPEARANCE;
ATTY. JOY J. CHEN OF WILSON ELSER & DICKER LLP, TEL. NO. 433-0990 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT NEURALTUS PHARMACEUTICALS, INC. BY TELEPHONE APPEARANCE.

RULING - A R G U E D; The Court adopted its tentative ruling. DEFENDANT UNIVERSITY OF IOWA PHARMACEUTICALS' Demurrer -The demurrer is overruled in its entirety. The Court has jurisdiction over the subject matter of each cause of action, and each cause of action sufficiently states a claim against Defendant UIP. This is a personal injury case brought by a California resident against defendants for personal injury that allegedly occurred in California when she opened a package containing a product allegedly manufactured by UIP. The Court assumes for purposes of this ruling that UIP is an entity of the State of Iowa and is covered by the Iowa Tort Claims Act, Iowa Code 669.1, et seq. (the "Act"). UIP contends that the Act requires that plaintiff file a governmental claim under the Iowa Act, and that the Iowa district courts are the exclusive jurisdiction for suits under that statute, even where a plaintiff is a non-Iowa resident and the act or omission occurred outside Iowa. Iowa Code 669.4. The Full Faith and Credit Clause does not require this Court to apply the Iowa Tort Claims Act and dismiss plaintiff's claims. (See Nevada v Hall, 440 U.S. 410 (1979).) Doing so would violate California's legitimate public policy of providing a forum for its resident plaintiff to seek redress for her alleged personal injuries to the extent they are caused by UIP. "[S]ister states who engage in activities in California are subject to our laws with respect to those activities and are subject to suit in California with respect to those activities." Hall v University of Nevada, 8 Cal. 3d 522, 524 (1972). California, its residents and taxpayers have a "substantial interest in providing a forum where a resident may seek whatever redress is done [her]." (Id. at 525) Further, "the presence of the evidence and witnesses in California could, of course, mean that plaintiffs if not permitted to proceed in California could find themselves seriously hampered in proving their case elsewhere." (Id. at 526.) Although UIP contends that California and Iowa's policies of filing government claims do not conflict, UIP fails to address the fact that UIP, by application of the Iowa statute, seeks to do more than require plaintiff to file a governmental claim; UIP seeks to require the California resident file her claim in a district court in Iowa. This conflicts with California's policy of providing a forum for Plaintiff here. Nor has Defendant shown why the principles of comity should apply here to give effect to the laws of Iowa and deny Plaintiff the opportunity to seek redress here. The application of comity suggested by Defendant UIP would not -- as UIP contends -- embody the same policy judgments as California's statute. (Prevailing party to prepare a form of order.) Judge: Marla J. Miller, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Court Reporter: Anthony C. Vaughn, CSR # 6185 =302/MJM
